mūla.devanāgarī: न कर्तृत्वं न कर्माणि लोकस्य सृजति प्रभुः | न कर्म-फल-संयोगं स्वभावस् तु प्रवर्तते
mūla.iast: na kartṛtvaṃ na karmāṇi lokasya sṛjati prabhuḥ | na karma-phala-saṃyogaṃ svabhāvas tu pravartate
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: रथघटप्रासादादीनि ईप्सिततमानि लोकस्य सृजति उत्पादयति प्रभुः आत्मा
देवाद्यसाधारणं कर्तृत्वं तत्तदसाधारणानि कर्माणि तत्तत् कर्म जन्यदेवादिफलसंयोगं
देहादेः कर्तृत्वं प्रभुरात्मा स्वामी न सृजति त्वं कुर्विति नियोगेन
। किन्तु तादृशः स्वभावो लोकनिष्ठः स्वत एव प्रवर्तते। अन्यथा परमात्मनो वैषम्यनैर्घृण्यप्रसङ्गः।
आत्मा। नापि रथादि कृतवतः तत्फलेन संयोगं न कर्मफलसंयोगम्। यदि किञ्चिदपि स्वतः न करोति न कारयति च देही कः तर्हि कुर्वन् क
अकर्मवश्यः स्वाभाविकस्वरूपेण अवस्थित आत्मा न सृजति नोत्पादयति
कारयति तं यमेभ्यो लोकेभ्य उन्निनीषत एष एवासाधु कर्म कारयति तं यमेभ्यो लोकेभ्योऽधो निनीषते इत्यादिश्रुतेः परमेश्वरेणैव श
कृतवतस्तत्फलसंबन्धं सृजति भोजयितापि भोक्तापि न भवतीत्यर्थः
इति उच्यते स्वभावस्तु स्वो भावः स्वभावः अविद्यालक्षणा प्रकृतिः माया प्रवर्तते दैवी हि इत्यादिना वक्ष्यमाणा
। अनाद्यविद्याकामवशात्प्रवृत्तिस्वभावं जीवलोकमीश्वरः कर्मसु नियुङ्क्ते न तु स्वयमेव कर्तृत्वादिकमुत्पादयतीत्यर्थः।

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: The sovereign (prabhuh, the ātman) neither fabricates agenthood (kartrtva) for the world, nor its desired objects — chariots, pots, palaces — nor the union of action with fruit; it is svabhāva (the nature that is māyā, avidyā) that moves. From the ultimate standpoint (paramārthatah), the ātman neither does nor causes anything: what appears as doing is entirely the projection of beginningless nescience. Liberation is the recognition that no real agenthood ever belonged to the self.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: The sovereign ātman — abiding in its own intrinsic form (svābhāvika-svarūpa), untouched by karma — does not generate the distinctive agenthood belonging to devas, animals, humans, or plants, nor their specific actions, nor their species-specific fruit-unions. What moves is svabhāva: the residual tendency (vāsanā) built up through beginningless prior karma that has conjoined the ātman with particular forms of prakṛti. Agenthood belongs to conditioned nature, not to the ātman's own essence.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: The jīva (prabhuh here read as the jīva, lord with respect to inert matter) does not in truth (vastutah) possess agenthood on its own: it is always dependent (jāda-apekṣya, dependent on inert instruments). Agency appears when the jīva misidentifies itself with the body-instrument complex, not as a property of the ātman as such. Hari alone is the ultimate ordainer; the jīva's apparent activity is fully contingent.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: The Paramātman (prabhu, Īśvara) does not author the agenthood, actions, or fruit-unions of the world of embodied beings — for Īśvara to do so would implicate Him in partiality (vaiṣamya) and cruelty (nairghṛṇya). Rather it is the svabhāva immanent in the world — the nature constituted of manifold womb-seeds and impressions (nānā-yoni-bīja-āśaya) — that moves of itself. The way out is not more karma but surrender of the ego's false agenthood: only then does the ātman's self-mastery (ātmaiva ātmano bandhuḥ) become operative.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: The verse answers an Upaniṣadic objection: since śruti says 'He (Paramīśvara) causes the worthy to perform good deeds and the unworthy to perform bad' (Kauṣītakī), is the jīva not simply compelled — and if so, how can it renounce action? Śrīdhara resolves this: Īśvara does not himself generate (na sṛjati) the jīva's agenthood, its actions, or its fruit-unions. Instead Īśvara sets in motion what is already the jīva's own svabhāva — the nature of avidyā and desire accumulated over beginningless time — without becoming its creator. The karmic weight rests entirely with the jīva.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana distinguishes three distinct negations with surgical precision: the ātman (prabhu, the inner sovereign) is neither the inducer of agenthood ('do this' — niyoga-kartā), nor the direct doer of desired objects (ghaṭa, etc.), nor the binder of the doer to fruit (bhojayitā-bhoktā). All three roles are svabhāva's — the divine māyā that appears as avidyā. As a sky does not actually become soiled by the cloud it seems to hold, the ātman is untouched. The upaniṣadic witness-self ('sa samānaḥ san ubhau lokāv anusañcarati... dhyāyatīva lelāyatīva') abides unchanged, luminous beneath every apparent motion.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: If no school assigns ultimate agenthood to the ātman, what exactly is held responsible when someone causes harm — and how does each school's answer shape its ethics of accountability?

Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita both invoke svabhāva but mean different things by it (cosmic māyā vs. accumulated karma-vāsanā); does this difference produce different prescriptions for change, or do they converge in practice?
Madhva reads prabhu as the jīva dependent on inert matter, while every other school reads it as Paramātman or ātman-as-sovereign; what is at stake theologically in this single lexical choice?

Śrīdhara is the only commentator to front-load an Upaniṣadic objection before resolving it; what does this dialogical structure reveal about how the bhakti-philological tradition reads scripture — as settled doctrine or live debate?

All six schools guard against the vaiṣamya-nairghṛṇya (divine partiality/cruelty) problem; does the fact that they converge on the same worry but diverge on the solution suggest the verse was deliberately composed as a multi-interpretable safeguard?

Vallabha alone explicitly cites BG 6.5 as interpretive context for 5.14; does reading the two verses together change what 'svabhāva pravar­tate' means — and what does this intertextual choice reveal about Puṣṭi-mārga hermeneutics?

Madhusūdana's sky-cloud analogy is exegetically generated, not in the mūla; at what point does a commentator's analogy become authoritative tradition — and how does this verse's reception history illustrate that process?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself rehearsing 'I did this, I caused that,' treat it as the smoke of avidyā (unknowing), not testimony. Practice tracing credit and blame back to the conditioned stream — body, habit, circumstance — without landing the weight on a fixed self. The goal is not apathy but the lightness that comes when the self stops defending a ledger it never actually owned.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Recognize that your characteristic style of acting — the way you instinctively lead, avoid, or help — is not 'you' but an accumulated vāsanā-pattern laid down by prior relationships, roles, and karmic encounter. This is not fatalism: the pattern is yours to offer as kainkarya (service) to Bhagavān rather than to hoard as personal identity. Redirect the habitual energy; do not merely suppress it.
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any moment of 'I am the one making this happen,' look for what inert structure you are leaning on — the body, the tool, the institution, the social role. Your agency is real but always borrowed and dependent. Cultivate the humility of a vessel: act fully, claim nothing, refer all effective power to Hari.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Stop auditing your own motives for purity before acting. The ego that watches itself act, judging whether it is being selfless enough, is itself the ahaṃkāra (ego-sense) that the verse is dissolving. Offer the action to Kṛṣṇa before, not after: the offering changes the action's metabolism, not its external form.
everyday_applications.bhakti: When you feel compelled by habit, appetite, or social pressure to act against your better judgment, do not immediately blame yourself or Providence. The verse names this precisely: svabhāva — your own accumulated nature — is what moves you. The task is not to erase svabhāva in one willful stroke but to bring it consciously into relationship with discernment (viveka), gradually loosening its autonomous hold.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Distinguish three distinct moments of ownership you attach to action: 'I directed this' (inducer), 'I produced this' (maker), 'I deserve this result' (enjoyer). Drop each separately. You need not wait until all three dissolve at once — even loosening one creates interior space. The śruti promise is that the witness-self, the ātman that 'seems to meditate, seems to tremble' (dhyāyatīva lelāyatīva), is already free; practice is simply stopping the re-narration.
